description.task: Write DDL (Data Definition Language) commands: CREATE, ALTER, and DROP to manage the structure of a table.
description.context: DDL commands define and modify the schema (structure) of the database. They include CREATE, ALTER, DROP, and TRUNCATE. Changes are auto-committed in most DBMS.
instructions: Run CREATE, INSERT, and first SELECT.
Run the ALTER ADD COLUMN command — re-run SELECT to see the new column.
Run the RENAME COLUMN command — verify column is now named 'notes'.
In a comment, list the 4 DDL commands and what each does (CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE).
Explain: why must TRUNCATE be used carefully compared to DELETE?
description.hint: TRUNCATE removes all rows instantly without logging each deletion — it cannot be rolled back in some DBMS. DELETE removes rows one at a time and can be rolled back inside a transaction.
description.expectedOutput:
After CREATE+INSERT: 3 rows
After ALTER ADD: same 3 rows with extra 'description' column (NULL)
After RENAME: columns are id, name, notes

description.task: Write DML (Data Manipulation Language) commands — INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE — to manage records in a table.
description.context: DML commands manipulate the data stored in tables. They can be wrapped inside transactions and rolled back if needed.
instructions: Run INSERT and the first SELECT — verify 5 rows.
Run the second SELECT — verify task 2 is 'done' and priorities of todo tasks increased.
Run DELETE — task 2 should be removed.
Run the final SELECT — 4 rows remain.
Add a comment explaining: what happens if you run DELETE FROM tasks; (no WHERE clause)?
description.hint: DELETE without WHERE deletes ALL rows in the table — but the table structure remains. Use TRUNCATE for that pattern. DROP TABLE removes the table itself.
description.expectedOutput:
After UPDATEs: task 2 status=done, todo tasks have priority incremented
After DELETE: 4 rows remain (task 2 removed)

description.task: Demonstrate TCL (Transaction Control Language) commands — COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT — and explain DCL (GRANT/REVOKE).
description.context: TCL commands manage transactions — grouping multiple DML statements so they either all succeed or all fail. DCL controls access permissions.
instructions: Run the BEGIN...COMMIT block with SAVEPOINT.
Run the final SELECT — orders 1 and 2 should be 'processing', order 3 still 'new'.
In a comment, list all 4 SQL command categories: DDL, DML, DCL, TCL with one example each.
In a comment, explain: what does GRANT SELECT ON orders TO 'reporting_user'; do (DCL example)?
description.hint: ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT undoes only the work done AFTER the savepoint. The transaction can then continue and ultimately COMMIT the earlier work.
description.expectedOutput:
Order 1: processing
Order 2: processing
Order 3: new (rolled back to savepoint)
